examples.explanation: Input: nums = [1,2,2], n = 3, total masks = 2^3 = 8. Mask 0 (000): subset = [] -> sorted [] -> add to set. Mask 1 (001): subset = [2] -> sorted [2] -> add. Mask 2 (010): subset = [2] -> sorted [2] -> duplicate, skip. Mask 3 (011): subset = [2,2] -> sorted [2,2] -> add. Mask 4 (100): subset = [1] -> sorted [1] -> add. Mask 5 (101): subset = [1,2] -> sorted [1,2] -> add. Mask 6 (110): subset = [1,2] -> sorted [1,2] -> duplicate, skip. Mask 7 (111): subset = [1,2,2] -> sorted [1,2,2] -> add. Final set: [[],[2],[2,2],[1],[1,2],[1,2,2]] -> convert to list.
Input: nums = [0], n = 1, total masks = 2^1 = 2. Mask 0 (0): subset = [] -> sorted [] -> add to set. Mask 1 (1): subset = [0] -> sorted [0] -> add. Final set: [[],[0]] -> convert to list.

approaches.name: Brute Force with Duplicate Check O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible subsets using bitmasking or recursion, then filter out duplicates by sorting and using a set. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to duplicate generation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique Subsets Using Brute Force with Duplicate Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of generating all subsets without duplicates using bitmasking and filtering duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Subsets II problem by generating all possible subsets and then removing duplicates. The intuition is that we can represent each subset as a binary mask where each bit corresponds to whether an element from the input array is included (1) or excluded (0). For an array of length n, there are 2^n possible masks. We iterate through all masks from 0 to 2^n - 1, build the corresponding subset, sort it to handle duplicates, and store it in a set to ensure uniqueness. Finally, we convert the set to a list. The brute force nature comes from generating all subsets regardless of duplicates, making it inefficient but straightforward. The time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because for each of the 2^n masks, we build a subset of up to n elements. The space complexity is O(n * 2^n) to store all subsets. This connects to L1 by using the key subroutine of generating subsets via bitmasking. Common mistakes include not sorting the subset before adding to the set (which fails to catch duplicates like [1,2] and [2,1]) or incorrectly handling empty subsets. The real code pattern involves iterating through bitmasks, building subsets, and using a set for deduplication.

examples.trace: Input: nums = [1,2,2], n = 3, total masks = 2^3 = 8. Mask 0 (000): subset = [] -> sorted [] -> add to set. Mask 1 (001): subset = [2] -> sorted [2] -> add. Mask 2 (010): subset = [2] -> sorted [2] -> duplicate, skip. Mask 3 (011): subset = [2,2] -> sorted [2,2] -> add. Mask 4 (100): subset = [1] -> sorted [1] -> add. Mask 5 (101): subset = [1,2] -> sorted [1,2] -> add. Mask 6 (110): subset = [1,2] -> sorted [1,2] -> duplicate, skip. Mask 7 (111): subset = [1,2,2] -> sorted [1,2,2] -> add. Final set: [[],[2],[2,2],[1],[1,2],[1,2,2]] -> convert to list.
Input: nums = [0], n = 1, total masks = 2^1 = 2. Mask 0 (0): subset = [] -> sorted [] -> add to set. Mask 1 (1): subset = [0] -> sorted [0] -> add. Final set: [[],[0]] -> convert to list.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Subsets Using Bitmasking
ladders.desc: Generate all possible subsets from an array using binary bitmask representation.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach, generating every subset by interpreting binary numbers as inclusion masks. The intuition is that each subset can be uniquely represented by a binary number where each bit position corresponds to an index in the input array: a 1 means include that element, and 0 means exclude it. For an array of size n, there are 2^n possible subsets (including the empty set). We iterate from 0 to 2^n - 1, and for each mask, we check each bit to build the subset. This works because binary counting covers all combinations of inclusion/exclusion. The algorithm: 1. Get n = length of nums. 2. Initialize an empty list 'all_subsets'. 3. For mask from 0 to 2^n - 1: a. Initialize an empty list 'subset'. b. For i from 0 to n-1: if the i-th bit of mask is 1, append nums[i] to subset. c. Append subset to all_subsets. 4. Return all_subsets. Time complexity is O(n * 2^n) as we iterate through 2^n masks and for each, check n bits. Space complexity is O(n * 2^n) for storing subsets. This feeds into L0 by providing the raw subsets that need deduplication. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges or incorrect bit checking (e.g., using (mask >> i) & 1). The real code pattern uses nested loops over masks and bits.

examples.trace: Input: nums = [1,2], n = 2, masks 0 to 3. Mask 0 (00): bits: 0->exclude 1, 1->exclude 2 -> subset []. Mask 1 (01): bits: 0->include 1, 1->exclude 2 -> subset [1]. Mask 2 (10): bits: 0->exclude 1, 1->include 2 -> subset [2]. Mask 3 (11): bits: 0->include 1, 1->include 2 -> subset [1,2]. All subsets: [[],[1],[2],[1,2]].
Input: nums = [3], n = 1, masks 0 to 1. Mask 0 (0): bit 0->exclude 3 -> subset []. Mask 1 (1): bit 0->include 3 -> subset [3]. All subsets: [[],[3]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Representing Subsets as Binary Masks
ladders.desc: Understand how binary numbers can encode inclusion/exclusion of elements in a subset.
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains the technique of using binary masks to represent subsets. The intuition is that a subset is defined by choosing which elements from the original set to include, and this choice can be mapped to a binary string: for n elements, use n bits where bit i is 1 if element i is included, and 0 otherwise. This creates a one-to-one correspondence between binary numbers from 0 to 2^n - 1 and all possible subsets. For example, with 3 elements, binary 101 (decimal 5) means include elements at indices 0 and 2. This works because binary counting enumerates all combinations of bits. The algorithm involves: 1. Determine n from the input array. 2. Recognize that each mask from 0 to 2^n - 1 represents a unique subset. 3. To extract the subset from a mask, iterate through bits and include elements where the bit is 1. The time complexity for generating one subset is O(n) to check bits, and space is O(n) for the subset. This feeds into L1 by providing the method to generate subsets from masks. Common mistakes include misunderstanding bit positions (e.g., LSB vs MSB ordering) or not handling empty masks (all zeros). The real code pattern uses bitwise operations like (mask >> i) & 1.

examples.trace: Input: array indices 0:A, 1:B, 2:C, mask = 6 (binary 110). Check bits: bit 0 (LSB): 0 -> exclude A. bit 1: 1 -> include B. bit 2: 1 -> include C. Resulting subset: [B,C] (note: order based on indices).
Input: array indices 0:X, 1:Y, mask = 1 (binary 01). Check bits: bit 0: 1 -> include X. bit 1: 0 -> exclude Y. Subset: [X].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through All Binary Numbers of Length n
ladders.desc: Generate all binary numbers from 0 to 2^n - 1 to use as masks.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating all possible binary numbers with n bits, which is essential for the mask-based subset generation. The intuition is that to explore all subsets of n elements, we need to consider every combination of including or excluding each element, which corresponds to all n-bit binary numbers. There are exactly 2^n such numbers, ranging from 0 (all bits 0, empty subset) to 2^n - 1 (all bits 1, full subset). We can generate these by simply counting from 0 to 2^n - 1 in decimal, as each integer has a unique binary representation. The algorithm: 1. Compute total_masks = 2^n (using left shift: 1 << n). 2. For i from 0 to total_masks - 1, treat i as a binary mask. This works because integer iteration covers all values. Time complexity is O(2^n) to iterate, and space is O(1) if not storing masks. This feeds into L2 by providing the masks to decode into subsets. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., going up to 2^n inclusive) or integer overflow for large n. The real code pattern uses a for-loop with range(1 << n).

examples.trace: Input: n = 2, total_masks = 2^2 = 4. Iterate i from 0 to 3: i=0 (binary 00), i=1 (01), i=2 (10), i=3 (11). All masks generated.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if a Specific Bit is Set in an Integer
ladders.desc: Determine whether a particular bit position is 1 or 0 in a binary representation of an integer.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is crucial for decoding binary masks into subsets. The intuition is that to build a subset from a mask, we need to check each bit position to decide whether to include the corresponding element. In binary, a bit is 'set' if it is 1. Given an integer mask and a bit position i (0-indexed from LSB), we can check if the i-th bit is set using bitwise operations. The standard method is: (mask >> i) & 1. This works by first shifting the mask right by i positions to bring the i-th bit to the LSB, then using AND with 1 to isolate it—if the result is 1, the bit is set; if 0, it is not. Alternatively, use mask & (1 << i) to check directly. The algorithm: 1. Input: integer mask, bit position i. 2. Compute bit_value = (mask >> i) & 1. 3. If bit_value == 1, bit is set; else, not set. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L3 by enabling the iteration through bits of each mask. Common mistakes include using 1-indexing for bits or incorrect shift direction. The real code pattern uses the expression (mask >> i) & 1.

examples.trace: Input: mask = 5 (binary 101), i=0. Compute: mask >> 0 = 5 (101), (5 & 1) = 1 (since 101 & 001 = 001). Result is 1, so bit is set.
Input: mask = 6 (binary 110), i=1. Compute: mask >> 1 = 3 (binary 011), (3 & 1) = 1 (011 & 001 = 001). Result is 1, so bit is set.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Binary Numbers and Bits
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of binary representation and bit positions in integers.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces binary numbers, which are essential for the bitmasking approach. The intuition is that computers represent integers in binary (base-2), using bits (binary digits) that are either 0 or 1. A binary number is a sequence of bits, where each bit position represents a power of 2. For example, the binary number 1011 represents 1*2^3 + 0*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 8+0+2+1 = 11 in decimal. Bits are typically indexed from right to left, starting at 0 for the least significant bit (LSB). Understanding this allows us to use integers as masks: each bit can indicate a choice (like including an element). The algorithm involves: 1. Recognize that an integer can be written in binary. 2. Identify bit positions: for an integer, bit i corresponds to the coefficient of 2^i. 3. To check bit i, consider the binary representation. Time and space are O(1) for basic operations. This feeds into L4 by providing the basis for bit checking. Common mistakes include confusion between binary and decimal or misindexing bits. The real code pattern involves thinking in terms of powers of 2.

examples.trace: Input: decimal number 5. Convert to binary: 5 = 4 + 1 = 2^2 + 2^0, so bits: position 2:1, position 1:0, position 0:1 → binary 101.
Input: binary number 110. Convert to decimal: bit positions from right: position 0:0, position 1:1, position 2:1. Compute: 0*2^0 + 1*2^1 + 1*2^2 = 0 + 2 + 4 = 6.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Sorting and Skipping O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Sort the input array first, then use backtracking to build subsets while skipping duplicate elements at the same level of recursion. This prevents duplicate subsets from being generated in the first place.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique Subsets Using Backtracking with Sorting and Skipping
ladders.desc: Complete solution to generate all subsets without duplicates using backtracking with sorting and duplicate skipping.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves Subsets II by sorting the array to group duplicates, then using a recursive 'Include or Skip' strategy. When we choose to skip an element, we must skip all subsequent occurrences of that same value at the current recursion level. This ensures that for any group of identical numbers, we only generate unique combinations (e.g., choosing two '2's, one '2', or zero '2's), effectively pruning duplicate branches from the recursion tree.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort input → [1,2,2].
Step 2: Start backtrack(0, []). i=0, value=1.
  - Choice 1: Include 1 → subset=[1], backtrack(1, [1]).
    - i=1, value=2.
      - Choice 1: Include 2 → subset=[1,2], backtrack(2, [1,2]).
        - i=2, value=2.
          - Choice 1: Include 2 → [1,2,2] added to result.
          - Choice 2: Skip 2 → [1,2] added to result.
      - Choice 2: Skip 2 (and skip the duplicate at i=2) → [1] added to result.
  - Choice 2: Skip 1 → backtrack(1, []).
    - i=1, value=2.
      - Choice 1: Include 2 → subset=[2], backtrack(2, [2]).
        - i=2, value=2.
          - Choice 1: Include 2 → [2,2] added to result.
          - Choice 2: Skip 2 → [2] added to result.
      - Choice 2: Skip 2 (and skip the duplicate at i=2) → [] added to result.
Final unique set: [[1,2,2], [1,2], [1], [2,2], [2], []].

Step 1: Sort input → [0].
Step 2: Start backtrack(0, []). i=0, value=0.
  - Choice 1: Include 0 → subset=[0], backtrack(1, [0]) → [0] added.
  - Choice 2: Skip 0 → backtrack(1, []) → [] added.
Final result: [[0], []].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Function with Duplicate Skipping
ladders.desc: The recursive backtracking function that builds subsets while skipping duplicates at the same level.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the backtracking with duplicate skipping. The intuition is that at each recursive call, we're at a specific position in the array and need to decide whether to include the current element in our subset. The key insight is that when we choose to skip an element, we should also skip all subsequent duplicate elements at that same decision level to avoid generating duplicate subsets. The function takes two parameters: the current index in the array and the current subset being built. First, we add the current subset to the result (this happens at every recursive call, generating subsets of all sizes). Then we iterate through the remaining elements starting from the given index. For each element, we include it in the subset and make a recursive call. After returning from the recursive call (backtracking), we remove the element and skip all duplicate elements at this level by incrementing the index while the next element equals the current one. This ensures that we don't generate subsets that differ only in which duplicate element was chosen. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(2^n) in the worst case (though typically less due to duplicates), and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. Common mistakes include adding the subset to results at the wrong time (should be at the beginning of each call), not properly skipping duplicates after the recursive call, and incorrect base case handling. The connection to L0 is that this function is the main recursive engine of the complete solution. The connection to L2 is that this implements the core logic of decision-making with duplicate avoidance.

examples.trace:
backtrack(0, []):
  1. Add [] to result.
  2. i=0 (val 1): Include 1 → current=[1], call backtrack(1, [1]).
     - Add [1] to result.
     - i=1 (val 2): Include 2 → current=[1,2], call backtrack(2, [1,2]).
       - Add [1,2] to result.
       - i=2 (val 2): Include 2 → current=[1,2,2], call backtrack(3, [1,2,2]).
         - Add [1,2,2] to result.
       - Backtrack: remove 2.
     - Backtrack: remove 2. Skip duplicate i=2 because nums[2]==nums[1].
  3. Backtrack: remove 1.
  4. i=1 (val 2): Include 2 → current=[2], call backtrack(2, [2]).
     - Add [2] to result.
     - i=2 (val 2): Include 2 → current=[2,2], call backtrack(3, [2,2]).
       - Add [2,2] to result.
     - Backtrack: remove 2.
  5. Backtrack: remove 2. Skip duplicate i=2 because nums[2]==nums[1].

backtrack(1, [1]):
  1. Add [1] to result.
  2. i=1 (val 1): Include 1 → current=[1,1], call backtrack(2, [1,1]).
     - Add [1,1] to result.
     - i=2 (val 2): Include 2 → current=[1,1,2], call backtrack(3, [1,1,2]).
       - Add [1,1,2] to result.
     - Backtrack: remove 2.
  3. Backtrack: remove 1. Skip duplicate i=2 if it were another 1.
  4. i=2 (val 2): Include 2 → current=[1,2], call backtrack(3, [1,2]).
     - Add [1,2] to result.
     - Backtrack: remove 2.

testCases.expected: Generates subsets starting at index 2 with [1,3]: [[1,3],[1,3,3]]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision Making with Duplicate Avoidance
ladders.desc: The central technique of making include/skip decisions while avoiding duplicate subsets.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core decision-making logic that prevents duplicate subsets. The intuition is that when we have duplicate values in a sorted array, we need to ensure that at each decision point in the recursion tree, we don't create multiple identical branches. The key insight is to treat duplicate elements specially: when we decide to include a particular value, we can include it multiple times (in different recursive calls at different depths), but when we decide to skip a value at a given level, we must skip all occurrences of that value at that same level. This works because in the recursion tree, each level corresponds to a decision about including elements starting from a particular index. If we skip element at index i (which has value x), and there's another element at index i+1 with the same value x, including that later element would create the same subsets as if we had included the first occurrence and skipped the second. By skipping all duplicates at the same level, we ensure each unique combination is generated exactly once. The algorithm at this level involves: 1) At each position, make the decision to include the current element. 2) After backtracking from the include decision, skip all subsequent elements with the same value. 3) Continue to the next distinct value. Time complexity analysis shows this reduces the branching factor when duplicates are present. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion depth. Common mistakes include skipping duplicates before making the include decision (which would prevent generating subsets with multiple copies of the same value), or not skipping enough duplicates. The connection to L1 is that this logic is implemented within the backtracking function's loop. The connection to L3 is that this decision-making relies on having a sorted array to identify duplicates efficiently.

examples.output: Decision process for element 2 at index 1
examples.trace:
At position i=1 with value=2:
Step 1: Include current element (2) → explore all subsets with this 2 included
Step 2: After returning from include branch, we're now at the 'skip' decision
Step 3: Since next element (index 2) also has value 2, we skip it too
Step 4: Move to next position (i=3, end of array)
This ensures we generate:
- Subsets with 0 occurrences of 2 (skipped both)
- Subsets with 1 occurrence of 2 (included first, skipped second)
- Subsets with 2 occurrences of 2 (included both)
But never generate the same subset twice (e.g., including the second 2 but not the first)

examples.output: Decision process for element 1 at index 0
examples.trace:
At position i=0 with value=1:
Step 1: Include current element (1) → explore subsets with at least one 1
Step 2: After returning, skip all duplicates at this level
  - Skip element at i=1 (value=1)
  - Skip element at i=2 (value=1)
Step 3: Move to i=3 (end)
This generates:
- Subsets with 0 ones (skip all)
- Subsets with 1 one (include first, skip rest)
- Subsets with 2 ones (include first two in different recursive calls)
- Subsets with 3 ones (include all)
Each combination generated exactly once

testCases.expected: Make include decision for first 2, then skip second 2
Include 5 or skip it (no duplicates to skip)
Include first 7, then skip next two 7s

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting to Enable Duplicate Identification
ladders.desc: Sorting the input array to group duplicate elements together.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses why we need to sort the array before applying the backtracking algorithm. The intuition is that for the duplicate skipping logic to work efficiently, we need duplicate elements to be adjacent to each other in the array. Sorting brings all identical values together, allowing us to identify and skip duplicates with a simple linear scan. Without sorting, duplicate values might be scattered throughout the array, making it impossible to implement the efficient skipping mechanism. The algorithm is straightforward: apply any O(n log n) sorting algorithm to the input array before starting the backtracking process. Common choices include quicksort, mergesort, or timsort (Python's default). After sorting, when we encounter an element and decide to skip it, we can easily skip all subsequent identical elements by checking adjacent array positions. Time complexity for this step is O(n log n), which is dominated by the O(n * 2^n) backtracking time, so it doesn't affect the overall asymptotic complexity. Space complexity depends on the sorting algorithm (O(1) for heapsort, O(n) for mergesort). Common mistakes include forgetting to sort altogether, sorting in descending order (which still works but might be confusing), or modifying the original array when it shouldn't be modified. The connection to L2 is that sorting enables the duplicate avoidance logic to work. The connection to L4 is that sorting relies on comparison operations between elements.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input array is [2,1,2].
Step 2: Apply a sorting algorithm. Initial comparison: 2 (index 0) and 1 (index 1). Since 2 > 1, swap them to get [1,2,2].
Step 3: Compare 2 (index 1) and 2 (index 2). They are equal, so their relative order remains unchanged.
Step 4: Final sorted array is [1,2,2]. Duplicates (the 2s) are now grouped together, allowing a single linear check to skip them during subset generation.

Step 1: Input array is [4,4,1,4].
Step 2: Begin sorting process. The smallest element (1) is moved to the front of the array.
Step 3: The remaining identical elements (4, 4, 4) are grouped together consecutively in the following indices.
Step 4: Final sorted result is [1,4,4,4]. Grouping all occurrences of 4 together is essential because it ensures that when the algorithm decides to 'skip 4', it can bypass all three instances at once.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Iterating through an array while tracking current position and skipping elements.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of traversing an array with index manipulation, which is essential for the backtracking algorithm. The intuition is that we need to systematically explore all elements of the array, making decisions at each position, and sometimes skipping certain elements based on conditions. The basic pattern involves a loop that iterates through array indices, with the ability to modify the loop variable to skip elements. In the context of our algorithm, we use a for loop where the index variable can be incremented within the loop body to skip duplicate elements. The algorithm: 1) Start with an index variable i. 2) Use a while or for loop to iterate while i is within array bounds. 3) At each position, perform some operation on array[i]. 4) Under certain conditions (like detecting a duplicate), increment i to skip elements. Time complexity for this operation is O(n) for a single traversal. Space complexity is O(1) for the index variables. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in index bounds, incorrectly updating the index within the loop (causing infinite loops or skipped elements), and not handling empty arrays. The connection to L3 is that this traversal is used after sorting to implement the duplicate skipping. The connection to L5 is that this builds on the basic concept of array indexing.

examples.trace:
Initialize loop at i = start (0).
- i=0: Process element 1. Loop continues.
- i=1: Process element 2. Loop continues.
- i=2: Check duplicate (nums[2] == nums[1]). Since i > start, this is a duplicate at the same level. Skip index 2.
- i=3: Process element 3. Loop continues.
End of loop.

Initialize loop at i = start (0).
- i=0: Process element 4. Loop continues.
- i=1: Check duplicate (nums[1] == nums[0]). i > start, so skip index 1.
- i=2: Check duplicate (nums[2] == nums[1]). i > start, so skip index 2.
End of loop. Only the first occurrence of '4' was used to start a new branch at this level.

testCases.expected: Process index 1, skip index 2, process index 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Subsets
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of arrays and what subsets are.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational level that introduces the basic concepts needed to understand the problem. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by index (position). For example, array [1,2,3] has three elements: element 0 is 1, element 1 is 2, element 2 is 3. A subset is a selection of elements from a set (or array), where order doesn't matter. For array [1,2], the subsets are: [] (empty subset), [1], [2], [1,2]. The key insight is that for an array of n elements, there are 2^n possible subsets (including the empty subset). This is because each element can either be included or excluded from the subset, giving 2 choices per element. With duplicates, some of these subsets may be identical. For example, for array [1,1], the subsets are: [], [1], [1,1]. Note that [1] (using the first 1) and [1] (using the second 1) are the same subset. Time complexity for enumerating all subsets is O(2^n) in general. Space complexity for storing all subsets is O(n * 2^n). Common beginner mistakes include confusing subsets with permutations (where order matters), not including the empty subset, and not understanding that [1,2] and [2,1] are the same subset. The connection to L4 is that arrays are the data structure we operate on, and subsets are what we're trying to generate.

examples.trace:
Element a: include or exclude (2 choices)
Element b: include or exclude (2 choices)
Total combinations: 2 × 2 = 4
List them:
1. Exclude a, exclude b → []
2. Include a, exclude b → [a]
3. Exclude a, include b → [b]
4. Include a, include b → [a,b]

Element x (first): include or exclude
Element x (second): include or exclude
Total combinations: 2 × 2 = 4
But some are identical:
1. Exclude both → []
2. Include first, exclude second → [x]
3. Exclude first, include second → [x] (same as #2)
4. Include both → [x,x]
So unique subsets: [], [x], [x,x]

approaches.name: Iterative Breadth-First Building O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Start with an empty subset and iteratively build subsets by adding elements while avoiding duplicates by checking if the current element is equal to the previous one and only adding to subsets created in the previous step. This builds subsets level by level.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique Subsets Using Iterative BFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of generating all subsets without duplicates using iterative breadth-first building.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Subsets II problem by building subsets iteratively while avoiding duplicates. The key insight is to sort the input array first, then process elements while handling duplicates carefully. We start with an empty subset and for each element in the sorted array, we create new subsets by adding the current element to existing subsets. For duplicate elements, we only add them to subsets created in the previous iteration to avoid generating duplicate subsets. This ensures each subset is unique while maintaining the BFS-like level-by-level expansion. The algorithm works by maintaining a result list that starts with just the empty subset. For each element at index i, we determine the starting index for copying existing subsets: if the current element equals the previous element, we only copy subsets created in the previous iteration (starting from where we began in the last iteration), otherwise we copy all existing subsets. Then we append the current element to each copied subset and add these new subsets to the result. This builds subsets in increasing size order while efficiently handling duplicates. Time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because we process n elements and potentially create 2^n subsets, with each subset copy taking O(n) time in worst case. Space complexity is O(n * 2^n) to store all subsets. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete iterative building process described in the key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to sort the array first, incorrectly handling duplicate elements by copying from wrong starting index, and not understanding why we need to track the starting index for duplicates. Real code pattern involves sorting, initializing result with empty list, then iterating through elements while tracking start index for duplicates.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort array → [1,2,2]
Step 2: Initialize result = [[]]
Step 3: Process element 1 (index 0):
  - Not duplicate, start = 0
  - Current result size = 1
  - For i from 0 to 0: copy result[0] = []
  - Add 1 → [1]
  - Append to result → [[],[1]]
Step 4: Process element 2 (index 1):
  - Not duplicate (previous element 1 ≠ 2), start = 0
  - Current result size = 2
  - For i from 0 to 1: copy [] and [1]
  - Add 2 → [2] and [1,2]
  - Append to result → [[],[1],[2],[1,2]]
Step 5: Process element 2 (index 2):
  - Duplicate (previous element 2 = 2), start = size_before_last_iteration = 2
  - Current result size = 4
  - For i from 2 to 3: copy [2] and [1,2]
  - Add 2 → [2,2] and [1,2,2]
  - Append to result → [[],[1],[2],[1,2],[2,2],[1,2,2]]
Final result contains all 6 unique subsets.

Step 1: Sort array → [0]
Step 2: Initialize result = [[]]
Step 3: Process element 0 (index 0):
  - Not duplicate, start = 0
  - Current result size = 1
  - For i from 0 to 0: copy result[0] = []
  - Add 0 → [0]
  - Append to result → [[],[0]]
Final result contains both empty subset and subset with single element.

ladders.title: Key Subroutine — Iterative Subset Building with Duplicate Handling
ladders.desc: The core iterative process that builds subsets while avoiding duplicates by tracking start index.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine implements the iterative building mechanism that forms the heart of the full solution. The intuition is to build subsets level by level (like BFS) while efficiently handling duplicate elements. We maintain a result list that grows as we process each element. For each new element, we need to decide which existing subsets to extend. The key insight: when we encounter a duplicate element, we should only extend subsets that were created in the previous iteration (when we last processed this same value). This prevents generating duplicate subsets. The algorithm works by first sorting the input array. We initialize result with just the empty subset. Then for each element in the sorted array, we determine how many existing subsets to copy: if current element equals previous element, we only copy subsets created since the last time we processed this element (starting from where we began last iteration), otherwise we copy all existing subsets. We then create new subsets by adding the current element to each copied subset, and append these to result. The start index tracking is crucial: we maintain a variable 'start' that indicates where to begin copying from. When element is not duplicate, start = 0. When duplicate, start = size of result before processing the previous element. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(2^n) in worst case as it builds all subsets. Space complexity is O(2^n) for storing subsets. This feeds into L0 as it's the main building block of the complete solution. Common mistakes include incorrect start index calculation for duplicates and not understanding why sorting is necessary. Real code pattern involves iterating through sorted array, tracking start index, and nested loop to copy and extend subsets.

examples.input: Sorted array [1,2,2], implement the iterative building
examples.trace:
Initialize: result = [[]], start = 0
Process 1: not duplicate, start = 0
  result_size = 1
  For i in [0,0]: copy result[0]=[], add 1 → [1]
  Append: result = [[],[1]]
Process 2: not duplicate (prev=1), start = 0
  result_size = 2
  For i in [0,1]: copy []→[2], [1]→[1,2]
  Append: result = [[],[1],[2],[1,2]]
Process 2: duplicate (prev=2), start = size_before_last = 2
  result_size = 4
  For i in [2,3]: copy [2]→[2,2], [1,2]→[1,2,2]
  Append: result = [[],[1],[2],[1,2],[2,2],[1,2,2]]

examples.input: Sorted array [1,1], implement the iterative building
examples.trace:
Initialize: result = [[]], start = 0
Process 1: not duplicate, start = 0
  result_size = 1
  For i in [0,0]: copy []→[1]
  Append: result = [[],[1]]
Process 1: duplicate (prev=1), start = size_before_last = 1
  result_size = 2
  For i in [1,1]: copy [1]→[1,1]
  Append: result = [[],[1],[1,1]]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Duplicates in Iterative Subset Generation
ladders.desc: The central technique of tracking start index to avoid duplicate subsets when processing consecutive identical elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic addresses the fundamental challenge in Subsets II: how to generate all subsets without duplicates when the input contains duplicate elements. The intuition is that when we have duplicate values in the sorted array, we must be careful about which existing subsets we extend with the duplicate element. If we extend all existing subsets with a duplicate element, we'll create duplicate subsets. The solution: when processing a duplicate element, only extend subsets that were created in the previous iteration (when we last processed this same value). This works because subsets created earlier already contain the previous instance of this value, and extending them again would create duplicates. The algorithm maintains a 'start' index that indicates where to begin copying existing subsets from. For a non-duplicate element, start = 0 (copy all subsets). For a duplicate element, start = size of result before processing the previous element (copy only subsets created since last time we saw this value). We need to track the result size before each iteration to calculate this correctly. This logic ensures each unique subset is generated exactly once. Time complexity for this logic is O(2^n) in worst case. Space complexity is O(1) for the tracking variables. This feeds into L1 as it's the key insight that enables correct duplicate handling. Common mistakes include forgetting to sort first (which makes duplicate detection impossible), incorrect calculation of start index, and not understanding why this logic prevents duplicates. Real code pattern involves comparing current element with previous element, updating start index based on whether they're equal, and using start to control which subsets are copied.

examples.input: Processing duplicate element 2 after previous element 2 in sorted array
examples.trace:
Scenario: result after processing first 2 = [[],[1],[2],[1,2]]
Size before processing first 2 was 2
Now processing second 2 (duplicate):
Compare current element (2) with previous (2) → equal
Therefore start = size_before_previous = 2
We only copy subsets from index 2 onward: [2] and [1,2]
Add 2 to create [2,2] and [1,2,2]
This avoids creating duplicate [2] (which would happen if we copied from index 0) and duplicate [1,2]

examples.input: Processing element 1 after previous element 2 (not duplicate)
examples.trace:
Scenario: result currently = [[],[2]]
Processing element 1:
Compare current element (1) with previous (2) → not equal
Therefore start = 0
We copy all existing subsets: [] and [2]
Add 1 to create [1] and [2,1]
Since 1 is new value, we need to extend all subsets to include it

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Subset Generation Without Duplicates
ladders.desc: Generating all subsets of a set iteratively (without handling duplicates yet).
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches the fundamental iterative approach to generating all subsets of a set. The intuition is that we can build subsets incrementally: start with the empty subset, then for each element in the input, create new subsets by adding that element to all existing subsets. This creates a breadth-first expansion where each iteration doubles the number of subsets (in the absence of duplicates). The algorithm works by initializing a result list with just the empty subset. Then for each element in the input array, we iterate through all current subsets in result, create a new subset by adding the current element to each existing subset, and append these new subsets to result. This generates all 2^n subsets for an n-element set. The process is iterative and builds subsets in increasing size order. Time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because we process n elements and for each element, we potentially iterate through up to 2^n subsets. Space complexity is O(n * 2^n) to store all subsets. This feeds into L2 as it's the base algorithm that needs to be enhanced with duplicate handling. Common mistakes include modifying the result list while iterating through it (which causes infinite loops), not creating proper copies of subsets before modifying them, and not understanding why this generates all subsets. Real code pattern involves nested loops: outer loop through elements, inner loop through current result size, creating new subsets by concatenation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result = [[]]
Step 2: Process element 1:
  Current result size = 1
  For i from 0 to 0: copy result[0] = []
  Add 1 → [1]
  Append to result → [[],[1]]
Step 3: Process element 2:
  Current result size = 2
  For i from 0 to 1: copy [] and [1]
  Add 2 → [2] and [1,2]
  Append to result → [[],[1],[2],[1,2]]
All 4 subsets generated.

Step 1: Initialize result = [[]]
Step 2: Process element 3:
  Current result size = 1
  For i from 0 to 0: copy result[0] = []
  Add 3 → [3]
  Append to result → [[],[3]]
Both subsets generated.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Creating New Subsets by Adding Element to Existing Subsets
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of extending existing subsets with a new element.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the core action in iterative subset generation: taking an existing subset and creating a new subset by adding a new element to it. The intuition is simple: if we have a subset S and an element x, the new subset is S ∪ {x} (all elements of S plus x). In implementation terms, this means creating a new list that contains all elements of the original subset plus the new element appended at the end. This operation must create a new list rather than modifying the original subset, because we need to preserve the original subset for other extensions. The algorithm involves: 1) Create a copy of the original subset (to avoid modifying it), 2) Append the new element to this copy, 3) The result is a new subset. This operation is performed repeatedly in nested loops to generate all subsets. Time complexity for a single operation is O(k) where k is the size of the original subset, as we need to copy all elements. Space complexity is O(k+1) for the new subset. This feeds into L3 as it's the atomic operation used in the iterative generation process. Common mistakes include modifying the original subset instead of creating a copy (which corrupts data), not understanding why copying is necessary, and inefficient copying methods. Real code pattern involves list concatenation or copy-and-append operations.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Original subset = [1,2]
Step 2: Create copy = [1,2] (new list with same elements)
Step 3: Append 3 → [1,2,3]
Step 4: Result is new subset [1,2,3]
Original subset [1,2] remains unchanged.

Step 1: Original subset = []
Step 2: Create copy = []
Step 3: Append 5 → [5]
Step 4: Result is new subset [5]
Original empty subset remains unchanged.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsets and Power Set
ladders.desc: The fundamental mathematical concept of subsets and the power set of a set.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces the basic mathematical concepts needed to understand subset generation problems. A subset is any collection of elements taken from a larger set, including the empty set (with no elements) and the set itself (all elements). For a set with n elements, there are exactly 2^n possible subsets. This collection of all subsets is called the power set. The intuition comes from combinatorial mathematics: for each element, we have two choices - include it in the subset or exclude it. With n independent choices, we have 2^n possibilities. For example, with set {1,2}, the subsets are: {} (neither included), {1} (include 1 only), {2} (include 2 only), and {1,2} (include both). This gives 2^2 = 4 subsets. The empty set {} is always a subset of any set. Sets are unordered collections, so {1,2} and {2,1} are the same subset. When dealing with lists/arrays in programming, we typically represent subsets as lists, often sorted for consistency. Understanding this fundamental concept is crucial for recognizing that subset generation problems require exploring all 2^n possibilities. Time and space complexity naturally relate to 2^n. This feeds into L4 as it explains why we need operations that generate all these possibilities. Common mistakes include forgetting the empty subset, counting duplicates as different subsets, and not understanding why there are 2^n subsets. Real code applications involve recognizing that subset problems require exponential algorithms.
